prompt: An authentic 16th-century antique map on aged, yellowed vellum, viewed strictly from a flat, top-down 2D orthographic perspective with zero 3D shading, zero volumetric rendering, and no isometric angles. The map depicts a rugged, highly fractal, jagged coastline drawn in flat black iron-gall ink outlines. Scattered in the water precisely tracking the intricate shore are historical depth soundings (small numbers). A traditional 32-point compass rose sits in the ocean, with straight, functional rhumb lines radiating perfectly outward from its exact center across the entire chart. Sweeping across the map is a historically plausible but stubbornly simplistic navigational route—a single, rigid, smooth curved line drawn in faded crimson ink. This red line represents an underfit model; it acts as a flawed navigational path that aggressively ignores the complex jaggedness of the coast and cuts straight through the numerical depth soundings without adjusting. Period-accurate Latin calligraphy labels coastal features, written perpendicular to the shoreline. Hand-tinted with muted watercolor washes of pale celadon and dull ochre. Pure cartographic grammar, no modern vector graphics.
tradition_approach: Targeted strict top-down orthographic perspective, functional rhumb lines radiating from a central 32-point compass rose, flat black ink outlines with zero volumetric shading, perpendicular coastal calligraphy, and period-accurate faded crimson ink to natively integrate the 'underfitted' mathematical arc as a flawed navigational route.
